From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This article is about the Swedish monarchy as an institution. For a list of kings and queens regnant of Sweden, see [List of Swedish monarchs](/wiki/List_of_Swedish_monarchs "List of Swedish monarchs"). For a list of Swedish princes and princesses, see [Swedish royal family](/wiki/Swedish_royal_family "Swedish royal family").

The **monarchy of Sweden** is centred on the monarchical [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"),[[1]](#cite_note-IOG1:5-1) by law a [constitutional](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and [hereditary monarchy](/wiki/Hereditary_monarchy "Hereditary monarchy") with a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system").[[2]](#cite_note-IOG1:1-2) There have been kings in what now is the [Kingdom of Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") for more than a millennium. Originally an [elective monarchy](/wiki/Elective_monarchy "Elective monarchy"), it became a [hereditary monarchy](/wiki/Hereditary_monarchy "Hereditary monarchy") in the 16th century during the reign of [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_Vasa "Gustav Vasa"),[[3]](#cite_note-Monarchy_in_Sweden-3) though virtually all monarchs before that belonged to a limited and small number of political families which are considered to be the royal dynasties of Sweden.

The official continuous count usually begins with the kings who ruled both [Svealand](/wiki/Svealand "Svealand") and [Götaland](/wiki/G%C3%B6taland "Götaland") as one kingdom.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Sweden's monarchy is amongst the oldest in the world, with a regnal list stretching back to the tenth century, starting with [Eric the Victorious](/wiki/Eric_the_Victorious "Eric the Victorious"); the Swedish monarchy has, for the past thousand years, undergone cycles of decline and strengthening, culminating in the modern constitutional monarchy.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The Swedish monarchy has been one of the key features in the development of Swedish culture, having for centuries patronized the arts and sciences. Several of Sweden's most prestigious academies and cultural institutions are under Swedish royal protection. This historical role politically, militarily and culturally, in spite of the country's otherwise liberal leanings, has resulted in the Swedish monarchy being popular.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In recent years, however, some of the most serious criticism ever published has taken place about the way his monarchy has developed under the current king's fifty-year reign.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-8)[[9]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Sweden in the present day is a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") in a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") based on [popular sovereignty](/wiki/Popular_sovereignty "Popular sovereignty"), as defined in the current [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Basic_Laws_of_Sweden#Instrument_of_Government "Basic Laws of Sweden") (one of the four [Basic Laws of the Realm](/wiki/Basic_Laws_of_Sweden "Basic Laws of Sweden") which makes up the written constitution[[11]](#cite_note-11)). The monarch and the members of the [royal family](/wiki/Swedish_royal_family "Swedish royal family") undertake a variety of official, unofficial and other representational duties within Sweden and abroad.[[3]](#cite_note-Monarchy_in_Sweden-3) The current king of Sweden is [Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Carl XVI Gustaf"), while his heir is [Crown Princess Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Crown_Princess_of_Sweden "Victoria, Crown Princess of Sweden").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The Swedish monarch has numerous residences, primarily state-owned but some privately owned; their official residence and workplace is [Stockholm Palace](/wiki/Stockholm_Palace "Stockholm Palace"), while [Drottningholm Palace](/wiki/Drottningholm_Palace "Drottningholm Palace") serves as the monarchy's private residence. Other notable residences include [Gripsholm Castle](/wiki/Gripsholm_Castle "Gripsholm Castle") and [Ulriksdal Palace](/wiki/Ulriksdal_Palace "Ulriksdal Palace"), as well as others throughout Sweden.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Several large palaces and a considerable section of the Swedish capital of [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") have been at the [disposal of the monarch](/wiki/Royal_Right_of_Disposal_(Sweden) "Royal Right of Disposal (Sweden)") since 1809.

### Pre-16th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-16th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Konungr.jpg/250px-Konungr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Konungr.jpg)

***kunuki***, i.e. *konungi*, the [dative case](/wiki/Dative_case "Dative case") for [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") *konungr* ("king"). A [runic](/wiki/Rune "Rune") inscription of the 11th century ([U11](/wiki/Uppland_Runic_Inscription_11 "Uppland Runic Inscription 11")) refers to King [Håkan the Red](/wiki/H%C3%A5kan_the_Red "Håkan the Red").

Scandinavian peoples have had kings since prehistoric times. As early as the 1st century CE, [Tacitus](/wiki/Tacitus "Tacitus") wrote that the [Suiones](/wiki/Suiones "Suiones") had a king, but the order of Swedish regnal succession up until King [Eric the Victorious](/wiki/Eric_the_Victorious "Eric the Victorious") (died 995), is known almost exclusively through accounts in historically controversial [Norse sagas](/wiki/Norse_saga "Norse saga") (see [Mythical kings of Sweden](/wiki/Mythical_kings_of_Sweden "Mythical kings of Sweden") and [Semi-legendary kings of Sweden](/wiki/Semi-legendary_kings_of_Sweden "Semi-legendary kings of Sweden")).

Originally, the Swedish king had combined powers limited to that of a war chief, a judge and a priest at the [Temple at Uppsala](/wiki/Temple_at_Uppsala "Temple at Uppsala") (see [Germanic king](/wiki/Germanic_king "Germanic king")). However, there are thousands of [runestones](/wiki/Runestone "Runestone") commemorating commoners, but no known chronicle[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] about the Swedish kings prior to the 14th century (though a list of kings was added in the [Västgöta Law](/wiki/V%C3%A4stg%C3%B6ta_Law "Västgöta Law")), and there is a relatively small number of runestones that are thought to mention kings: Gs 11 ([Emund the Old](/wiki/Emund_the_Old "Emund the Old") – reigned 1050–1060), [U 11](/wiki/Uppland_Runic_Inscription_11 "Uppland Runic Inscription 11") ([Håkan the Red](/wiki/H%C3%A5kan_the_Red "Håkan the Red") – late 11th century) and [U 861](/wiki/Norsta_Runestone "Norsta Runestone") ([Blot-Sweyn](/wiki/Blot-Sweyn "Blot-Sweyn") – reigned c. 1080).

About 1000 A.D., the first king known to rule both [Svealand](/wiki/Svealand "Svealand") and [Götaland](/wiki/G%C3%B6taland "Götaland") was [Olof Skötkonung](/wiki/Olof_Sk%C3%B6tkonung "Olof Skötkonung"), but further history for the next two centuries is obscure, with many kings whose tenures and actual influence/power remains unclear. The Royal Court of Sweden, however, does count Olof's father, Eric the Victorious, as Sweden's first king. The power of the king was greatly strengthened[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] by the introduction of Christianity during the 11th century, and the following centuries saw a process of consolidation of power into the hands of the king. The Swedes traditionally elected a king from a favored dynasty at the [Stones of Mora](/wiki/Stones_of_Mora "Stones of Mora"), and the people had the right to elect the king as well as to depose him. The ceremonial stones were destroyed around 1515.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 12th century, the consolidation of Sweden was still affected by dynastic struggles between the [Erik](/wiki/House_of_Erik "House of Erik") and [Sverker](/wiki/House_of_Sverker "House of Sverker") clans, which ended when a third clan married into the Erik clan and the [House of Bjälbo](/wiki/House_of_Bj%C3%A4lbo "House of Bjälbo") was established on the throne. That dynasty formed pre-[Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union") Sweden into a strong state, and finally King [Magnus Eriksson](/wiki/Magnus_Eriksson "Magnus Eriksson") (reigned 1319–1364) even ruled [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") (1319–1343) and [Scania](/wiki/Scania "Scania") (1332–1360). Following the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"),[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] the union weakened, and Scania was captured by Denmark.

In 1397, after the Black Death and domestic power struggles, Queen [Margaret I of Denmark](/wiki/Margaret_I_of_Denmark "Margaret I of Denmark") united Sweden (then including [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")), Denmark and Norway (then including [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland")) in the [Union of Kalmar](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union") with the approval of the [Swedish nobility](/wiki/Swedish_nobility "Swedish nobility"). Continual tension within each country and the union led to open conflict between the Swedes and the Danes in the 15th century. The union's final disintegration in the early 16th century led to prolonged rivalry between Denmark-Norway and Sweden (with Finland) for centuries to come.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Gustav_Vasa.jpg/250px-Gustav_Vasa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gustav_Vasa.jpg)

[Gustav I](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden"), portrayed here in 1542 by [Jakob Binck](/wiki/Jakob_Binck "Jakob Binck"), legally created the hereditary monarchy and organized the Swedish [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state").

Catholic bishops had supported the [King of Denmark](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Denmark "Monarchy of Denmark"), [Christian II](/wiki/Christian_II_of_Denmark "Christian II of Denmark"), but he was [overthrown in a rebellion](/wiki/Swedish_War_of_Liberation "Swedish War of Liberation") led by nobleman [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden"), whose father had been executed at the [Stockholm bloodbath](/wiki/Stockholm_bloodbath "Stockholm bloodbath"). Gustav Vasa (hereinafter referred to as Gustav I) was elected King of Sweden by the [estates of the realm](/wiki/Estates_of_the_realm "Estates of the realm"), assembled in [Strängnäs](/wiki/Str%C3%A4ngn%C3%A4s "Strängnäs") on 6 June 1523.

Inspired by the teachings of [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther"), Gustav I used the Protestant [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") to curb the power of the Roman [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). In 1527 he persuaded the [estates of the realm](/wiki/Estates_of_the_realm "Estates of the realm"), assembled in the city of [Västerås](/wiki/V%C3%A4ster%C3%A5s "Västerås"), to confiscate church lands, which comprised 21% of the country's farmland. At the same time, he broke with the [papacy](/wiki/Pope "Pope") and established a reformed [state church](/wiki/State_church "State church"): the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden").[[n 1]](#cite_note-n2-14) Throughout his reign, Gustav I suppressed both aristocratic and [peasant opposition](/wiki/Dacke_War "Dacke War") to his ecclesiastical policies and efforts at centralisation, which to some extent laid the foundation for the modern Swedish [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"). Legally Sweden has only been a [hereditary monarchy](/wiki/Hereditary_monarchy "Hereditary monarchy") since 1544 when the [Riksdag of the Estates](/wiki/Riksdag_of_the_Estates "Riksdag of the Estates"), through *Västerås arvförening*, designated the sons of King Gustav I as the heirs to the Throne.[[n 2]](#cite_note-n3-15)

Tax reforms took place in 1538 and 1558, whereby multiple complex taxes on independent farmers were simplified and standardised throughout the district[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and tax assessments per farm were adjusted to reflect ability to pay. Crown tax revenues increased, but more importantly the new system was perceived as fairer. A war with [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck") in 1535 resulted in the expulsion of the [Hanseatic traders](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League"), who previously had had a monopoly on foreign trade. With its own burghers in charge, Sweden's economic strength grew rapidly, and by 1544 Gustav controlled 60% of the farmlands in all of Sweden. Sweden now built the first modern army in Europe, supported by a sophisticated tax system and an efficient bureaucracy.[[14]](#cite_note-16)

At the death of King Gustav I in 1560, he was succeeded by his oldest son [Eric XIV](/wiki/Eric_XIV_of_Sweden "Eric XIV of Sweden"). His reign was marked by Sweden's entrance into the [Livonian War](/wiki/Livonian_War "Livonian War") and the [Northern Seven Years' War](/wiki/Northern_Seven_Years%27_War "Northern Seven Years' War"). The combination of Eric's developing [mental disorder](/wiki/Mental_disorder "Mental disorder") and his opposition to the aristocracy led to the [Sture Murders](/wiki/Sture_Murders "Sture Murders") in 1567 and the imprisonment of his brother [John (III)](/wiki/John_III_of_Sweden "John III of Sweden"), who was married to [Catherine Jagiellon](/wiki/Catherine_Jagiellon "Catherine Jagiellon"), sister of King [Sigismund II of Poland](/wiki/Sigismund_II_of_Poland "Sigismund II of Poland").[[15]](#cite_note-17) In 1568 Eric was dethroned and succeeded by John III. In domestic politics John III showed clear [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") sympathies, inspired by his queen, creating friction with the Swedish clergy and nobility. He reintroduced several Catholic traditions previously abolished, and his foreign policy was affected by his family connection to the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), where his eldest son had been made King [Sigismund III](/wiki/Sigismund_III_Vasa "Sigismund III Vasa") in 1587.[[n 3]](#cite_note-n20-18) Following the death of his father, Sigismund tried to [rule Sweden from Poland](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Swedish_union "Polish–Swedish union"), leaving Sweden under the control of a [regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent") – his paternal uncle (Gustav I's youngest son) [Charles (IX)](/wiki/Charles_IX_of_Sweden "Charles IX of Sweden") – but was unable to defend his Swedish throne against the ambitions of his uncle. In 1598 Sigismund and his Swedish-Polish army were defeated at the [Battle of Stångebro](/wiki/Battle_of_St%C3%A5ngebro "Battle of Stångebro") by the forces of Charles, and he was declared deposed by the [Estates](/wiki/Riksdag_of_the_Estates "Riksdag of the Estates") in 1599.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Gustavus_Adolphus_at_the_Battle_at_Breitenfeld.jpg/250px-Gustavus_Adolphus_at_the_Battle_at_Breitenfeld.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gustavus_Adolphus_at_the_Battle_at_Breitenfeld.jpg)

*The Lion of the North*: King [Gustavus Adolphus](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus "Gustavus Adolphus") depicted at the turning point of the [Battle of Breitenfeld (1631)](/wiki/Battle_of_Breitenfeld_(1631) "Battle of Breitenfeld (1631)") against the forces of [Johann Tserclaes, Count of Tilly](/wiki/Johann_Tserclaes,_Count_of_Tilly "Johann Tserclaes, Count of Tilly")

In 1604, the Estates finally recognized the regent and de facto ruler as King Charles IX. His short reign was one of uninterrupted warfare. The hostility of Poland and the breakup of Russia involved him in overseas contests for the possession of [Livonia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Livonia_(1629%E2%80%931721) "Duchy of Livonia (1629–1721)") and [Ingria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Ingria "Duchy of Ingria"), the [Polish–Swedish War (1600–1611)](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Swedish_War_(1600%E2%80%931611) "Polish–Swedish War (1600–1611)") and the [Ingrian War](/wiki/Ingrian_War "Ingrian War"), while his pretensions to claim [Lapland](/wiki/Laponia_(historical_province) "Laponia (historical province)") brought on a war with [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") ([Kalmar War](/wiki/Kalmar_War "Kalmar War")) in the last year of his reign.[[n 4]](#cite_note-n1-19)

[Gustavus Adolphus](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus "Gustavus Adolphus") inherited three wars from his father when he ascended to the throne. From 1612, when Count [Axel Oxenstierna](/wiki/Axel_Oxenstierna "Axel Oxenstierna") was appointed [Lord High Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_High_Chancellor_of_Sweden "Lord High Chancellor of Sweden"), which he remained until Gustavus Adolphus's death, the two men struck a long and successful partnership and complemented each other well: In Oxenstierna's own words, his "cool" balanced the King's "heat".[[16]](#cite_note-Trettioåriga_kriget-20)[[17]](#cite_note-Nordisk_Familjebok_-_Axel_Gustafsson_Oxenstierna-21) The war against Russia (the [Ingrian War](/wiki/Ingrian_War "Ingrian War")) ended in 1617 with the [Treaty of Stolbovo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Stolbovo "Treaty of Stolbovo"), which excluded Russia from the Baltic Sea. The final inherited war, the [war against Poland](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Swedish_War_(1600%E2%80%931629) "Polish–Swedish War (1600–1629)"), ended in 1629 with the [Truce of Altmark](/wiki/Truce_of_Altmark "Truce of Altmark"), which transferred the large province of [Livonia](/wiki/Swedish_Livonia "Swedish Livonia") to Sweden and freed the Swedish forces for subsequent intervention in the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") in Germany, where Swedish forces had [already established a bridgehead in 1628](/wiki/Siege_of_Stralsund_(1628) "Siege of Stralsund (1628)"). [Brandenburg](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Brandenburg "Margraviate of Brandenburg") was torn apart by a quarrel between the Protestants and the Catholics. When Gustavus Adolphus [began his push](/wiki/Treaty_of_Stettin_(1630) "Treaty of Stettin (1630)") into northern Germany in June–July 1630, he had just 4,000 soldiers. But he was soon able to consolidate the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") position in the north, using reinforcements from Sweden and money supplied by France at the [Treaty of Bärwalde](/wiki/Treaty_of_B%C3%A4rwalde "Treaty of Bärwalde").[[18]](#cite_note-22)[[n 5]](#cite_note-n17-23) Gustavus Adolphus was killed at the [1632 Battle of Lützen](/wiki/Battle_of_L%C3%BCtzen_(1632) "Battle of Lützen (1632)"). Queen [Maria Eleonora](/wiki/Maria_Eleonora_of_Brandenburg "Maria Eleonora of Brandenburg") and the [king's ministers](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_Sweden "Privy Council of Sweden") took over the government of the Realm on behalf of Gustavus Adolphus' underage daughter [Christina](/wiki/Christina_I_of_Sweden "Christina I of Sweden"), until she reached the [age of majority](/wiki/Age_of_majority "Age of majority"). Gustavus Adolphus is often regarded by military historians as one of the greatest military commanders of all time, with innovative use of [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms"). [[n 6]](#cite_note-n8-24)

[Christina](/wiki/Christina,_Queen_of_Sweden "Christina, Queen of Sweden") succeeded her father aged six. A regency government ruled in her name until she turned 18 years of age. During the regency, Chancellor Axel Oxenstierna wrote the [1634 Instrument of Government](/wiki/1634_Instrument_of_Government "1634 Instrument of Government"), which although never approved by any monarch, continued to have an important normative role in the state administration. Christina early on showed an interest in literature and the sciences and famously brought [René Descartes](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Descartes "René Descartes") to Sweden. Sweden continued to be involved in the Thirty Years' War during reign of Christina and that conflict was settled at the 1648 [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia"), and the Swedish monarch received representation at the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Imperial Diet (Holy Roman Empire)") due to the German conquests ([Bremen-Verden](/wiki/Bremen-Verden "Bremen-Verden") and [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania")) that were made.[[n 7]](#cite_note-n10-25) Having decided not to marry, Christina abdicated the throne on 5 June 1654 in favor of her cousin [Charles X Gustav](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav "Charles X Gustav"), went abroad, and converted to [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism").

[Charles XI](/wiki/Charles_XI_of_Sweden "Charles XI of Sweden") at the [Battle of Lund](/wiki/Battle_of_Lund "Battle of Lund") in 1676. Painting by [David Klöcker Ehrenstrahl](/wiki/David_Kl%C3%B6cker_Ehrenstrahl "David Klöcker Ehrenstrahl").

The Estates elected [Charles X Gustav](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav "Charles X Gustav") as their new King, and his short reign is best characterized by [foreign wars](/wiki/Second_Northern_War "Second Northern War"): [first a lengthy campaign within Poland](/wiki/Deluge_(history) "Deluge (history)") and then with Denmark. In the latter case, the risky 1658 [March across the Belts](/wiki/March_across_the_Belts "March across the Belts") which resulted in the [Treaty of Roskilde](/wiki/Treaty_of_Roskilde "Treaty of Roskilde"), would prove to be the largest permanent territorial gain Sweden ever had: [Skåne](/wiki/Sk%C3%A5ne "Skåne"), [Blekinge](/wiki/Blekinge "Blekinge") and [Bohuslän](/wiki/Bohusl%C3%A4n "Bohuslän") now became Swedish provinces and have remained so ever since. Charles X Gustav was not satisfied, as he wanted to crush Denmark once and for all, but the [1659 Assault on Copenhagen](/wiki/Assault_on_Copenhagen_(1659) "Assault on Copenhagen (1659)") did not prove successful for the Swedes, largely due to the Dutch [naval intervention](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Sound "Battle of the Sound") to the aid of the Danes.

Charles X Gustav died in [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg") in 1660 and as the Crown passed to his five-year-old son [Charles XI](/wiki/Charles_XI_of_Sweden "Charles XI of Sweden"), a new regency government would assume the responsibilities of the state. The regency government, composed of aristocrats and led by Chancellor Count [Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie](/wiki/Magnus_Gabriel_De_la_Gardie "Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie"), was more interested in feathering their own nests rather than working in the interest of the country at large. When Charles XI came of age in 1672, the effectiveness of the armed forces had seriously deteriorated and the country was ill-prepared as the King of Denmark, [Christian V](/wiki/Christian_V_of_Denmark "Christian V of Denmark"), [invaded to settle old scores](/wiki/Scanian_War "Scanian War"). The Danes were [ultimately unsuccessful](/wiki/Peace_of_Lund "Peace of Lund") in their attempts, and Charles XI undertook several measures to prevent what had just almost happened from occurring again: reducing the influence of the aristocracy by [nationalizing estates and properties](/wiki/Reduction_(Sweden) "Reduction (Sweden)") which had been handed out to them by his predecessors, introducing the [Allotment system](/wiki/Allotment_system "Allotment system") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *indelningsverket*) which would form the basis of the armed forces until the 20th century, and with the support of the Estates he was declared in 1680 an [absolute monarch](/wiki/Absolute_monarch "Absolute monarch").

Charles XI was succeeded by his son, [Charles XII](/wiki/Charles_XII_of_Sweden "Charles XII of Sweden"), who would prove to be an extremely able military commander, defeating far larger enemies with the small but highly professional Swedish army. His defeat of the Russians [at Narva](/wiki/Battle_of_Narva_(1700) "Battle of Narva (1700)") when just 18 years old was to be his greatest victory. However his campaigning at the head of his army during the [Great Northern War](/wiki/Great_Northern_War "Great Northern War") would ultimately lead to catastrophic defeat at the [Battle of Poltava](/wiki/Battle_of_Poltava "Battle of Poltava") after which he spent several years in [Turkey (now Moldova)](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"). Some years later he was killed at the [Siege of Fredriksten](/wiki/Siege_of_Fredriksten "Siege of Fredriksten") during an attempt to invade Norway. The Swedish Age of Greatness ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *stormaktstiden*) had ended.[[n 8]](#cite_note-n18-26)

Charles XII's sister, [Ulrika Eleonora](/wiki/Ulrika_Eleonora,_Queen_of_Sweden "Ulrika Eleonora, Queen of Sweden"), now claimed the throne over her nephew and son of her elder sister, [Charles Frederick, Duke of Holstein-Gottorp](/wiki/Charles_Frederick,_Duke_of_Holstein-Gottorp "Charles Frederick, Duke of Holstein-Gottorp") (see genealogy chart above). Charles Frederick had the claim of seniority within the family, but Ulrica Eleonora claimed that her elder sister had not "acquired the consent of the Parliamentary [Estates](/wiki/Estates_of_the_realm#In_Sweden_and_Finland "Estates of the realm")" for her marriage to his father, according to laws of succession laid down in *Norrköpings arvförening*. The duke's party asserted that the [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") in Sweden, which his grandfather King Charles XI had created, made that marriage clause irrelevant. When Charles Frederick was confronted with Ulrika Eleonora, he was forced by [Arvid Horn](/wiki/Arvid_Horn "Arvid Horn") to greet her as queen.[[19]](#cite_note-Lundh-Eriksson-27) He asked to be granted the title Royal Highness and to be recognised as her heir, but when her husband, [Frederick of Hesse](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Sweden "Frederick I of Sweden"), instead was given the title, he left Sweden in 1719. In 1723, he was granted the title Royal Highness in his absence, but his pro-Russian policy at that time made him impossible as heir to the Swedish throne. His marriage in 1725 to [Anna](/wiki/Grand_Duchess_Anna_Petrovna_of_Russia "Grand Duchess Anna Petrovna of Russia"), the daughter of Peter of Great, did not help his case.[[19]](#cite_note-Lundh-Eriksson-27) His mother, and later Hedwig Eleonora, both supported and worked for his right to be considered heir of Sweden after his childless uncle.[[19]](#cite_note-Lundh-Eriksson-27)

[Ulrika Eleonora](/wiki/Ulrika_Eleonora,_Queen_of_Sweden "Ulrika Eleonora, Queen of Sweden") was forced by the Estates to sign the [1719 Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_(1719) "Instrument of Government (1719)"), which ended the absolute monarchy and made the [Riksdag of the Estates](/wiki/Riksdag_of_the_Estates "Riksdag of the Estates") the highest organ of the state and reduced the role of monarch to a [figurehead](/wiki/Figurehead "Figurehead"). The [Age of Liberty](/wiki/Age_of_Liberty "Age of Liberty") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *frihetstiden*) with its parliamentary rule, dominated by two parties – the [Caps](/wiki/Caps_(party) "Caps (party)") and the [Hats](/wiki/Hats_(party) "Hats (party)") – had begun. Ulrika Eleonora had had enough after a year on the throne and abdicated in favor of her husband, [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Sweden "Frederick I of Sweden"), who had little interest in the affairs of state and was elected King by the Estates as King Frederick I, resulting in the [1720 Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_(1720) "Instrument of Government (1720)"): content-wise almost identical to the one from 1719. Despite having many extra-marital affairs, Frederick I never sired a legitimate heir to the throne.

After the death/impending death of King Frederick without heirs, Charles Frederick's heir, Charles Peter Ulrich, had become untenable in Sweden, as he had been taken to Russia by his aunt [Elizabeth I of Russia](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_Russia "Elizabeth I of Russia"), nominated as heir/Grand Duke, and became Emperor [Peter III of Russia](/wiki/Peter_III_of_Russia "Peter III of Russia"). In 1743, Adolf Frederick, a cousin of Charles Frederick, of the same house of Holstein-Gottrop, and a descendant of a sister of [Charles X Gustav of Sweden](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav_of_Sweden "Charles X Gustav of Sweden") was elected heir to the throne of Sweden by the [Hat faction](/wiki/Hats_(party) "Hats (party)") (Swedish: *Hattarna*). The Hat faction wanted to obtain better conditions at the [Treaty of Åbo](/wiki/Treaty_of_%C3%85bo "Treaty of Åbo") from Empress [Elizabeth of Russia](/wiki/Elizabeth_of_Russia "Elizabeth of Russia"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] who had adopted his nephew as her [heir](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive"). His mother, [Albertina Frederica of Baden-Durlach](/wiki/Albertina_Frederica_of_Baden-Durlach "Albertina Frederica of Baden-Durlach") (1682–1755), was a descendant of earlier royal dynasties of Sweden, great-granddaughter of [Princess Catherine of Sweden](/wiki/Catherine_of_Sweden,_Countess_Palatine_of_Kleeburg "Catherine of Sweden, Countess Palatine of Kleeburg"), mother of King [Charles X of Sweden](/wiki/Charles_X_of_Sweden "Charles X of Sweden").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] On his mother's side, Adolf Frederick was descended from King [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden") and [Christina Magdalena](/wiki/Christina_Magdalena_of_Pfalz-Zweibr%C3%BCcken "Christina Magdalena of Pfalz-Zweibrücken"), a sister of [Charles X of Sweden](/wiki/Charles_X_of_Sweden "Charles X of Sweden").[[20]](#cite_note-28) He succeeded as King Adolf Frederick 8 years later on 25 March 1751.[[21]](#cite_note-29)

During his 20-year reign, Adolf Frederick was little more than a [figurehead](/wiki/Figurehead "Figurehead"), the real power being with the [Riksdag of the Estates](/wiki/Riksdag_of_the_Estates "Riksdag of the Estates"), often distracted by party strife. Twice he endeavored to free himself from the tutelage of the estates. The first occasion was in 1756. Stimulated by his consort [Louisa Ulrika of Prussia](/wiki/Louisa_Ulrika_of_Prussia "Louisa Ulrika of Prussia") (sister of [Frederick the Great](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great")), he tried to regain a portion of the attenuated prerogative through the [Coup of 1756](/wiki/Coup_of_1756 "Coup of 1756") to abolish the rule of the Riksdag of the Estates and reinstate absolute monarchy in Sweden. He nearly lost his throne in consequence. On the second occasion during the [December Crisis of 1768](/wiki/December_Crisis_(1768) "December Crisis (1768)"), under the guidance of his eldest son, [Gustav](/wiki/Gustav_III "Gustav III"), he succeeded in overthrowing the "Cap" (Swedish: *Mössorna*) senate, but was unable to make any use of his victory.[[22]](#cite_note-30)

Adolf Frederick's son, King [Gustav III](/wiki/Gustav_III "Gustav III"), was more successful in restoring royal authority. In 1772, the 1720 Instrument in Government was later replaced by the [1772 Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_(1772) "Instrument of Government (1772)") in a [self-coup](/wiki/Self-coup "Self-coup") orchestrated by the King.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Karl_XIV_Johan%2C_king_of_Sweden_and_Norway%2C_painted_by_Fredric_Westin.jpg/250px-Karl_XIV_Johan%2C_king_of_Sweden_and_Norway%2C_painted_by_Fredric_Westin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_XIV_Johan,_king_of_Sweden_and_Norway,_painted_by_Fredric_Westin.jpg)

Crown Prince [Charles John](/wiki/Charles_XIV_John_of_Sweden "Charles XIV John of Sweden") at the [Battle of Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig") (1813). Painting by [Fredric Westin](/wiki/Fredric_Westin "Fredric Westin").

On 17 September 1809 in the [Treaty of Fredrikshamn](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fredrikshamn "Treaty of Fredrikshamn"), as a result of the poorly managed [Finnish War](/wiki/Finnish_War "Finnish War"), Sweden had to surrender [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") to Russia. King [Gustav IV Adolf](/wiki/Gustav_IV_Adolf "Gustav IV Adolf") and his descendants were deposed in a coup d'état led by dissatisfied [army](/wiki/Swedish_Army "Swedish Army") officers. The childless uncle of the former king was almost immediately elected as King [Charles XIII](/wiki/Charles_XIII_of_Sweden "Charles XIII of Sweden"). The [Instrument of Government of 1809](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_(1809) "Instrument of Government (1809)") put an end to royal [absolutism](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") by dividing the legislative power between the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag_of_the_Estates "Riksdag of the Estates") (primary) and the king (secondary), and vested executive power in the king when acting through the [Council of State](/wiki/King_in_Council_(Sweden) "King in Council (Sweden)").

The present [Bernadotte dynasty](/wiki/House_of_Bernadotte "House of Bernadotte") was established in September 1810 when the Riksdag, convened in [Örebro](/wiki/%C3%96rebro "Örebro"), elected [French Marshal](/wiki/Marshal_of_France "Marshal of France") and Prince of Pontecorvo [Jean-Baptiste Jules Bernadotte](/wiki/Charles_XIV_John_of_Sweden "Charles XIV John of Sweden") as crown prince. This took place because Charles XIII had no legitimate heir, and a crown prince previously elected in January 1810, [Charles August](/wiki/Charles_August,_Crown_Prince_of_Sweden "Charles August, Crown Prince of Sweden"), suddenly had died of a stroke during a military exercise.

Although the 19th century Bernadotte monarchs that would follow [Charles XIV John](/wiki/Charles_XIV_John "Charles XIV John")'s reign tried to defend the power and privileges they still had, the tide incrementally turned against "personal regal rule" ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *personlig kungamakt*) with the growth of the liberals, social democrats, and the expansion of the franchise.[[23]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:66-67-31)

The daughter of [Gustav IV Adolph](/wiki/Gustav_IV_Adolph "Gustav IV Adolph"), Princess [Sofia Wilhelmina](/wiki/Princess_Sophie_of_Sweden "Princess Sophie of Sweden") (21 May 1801 – 1865) married Grand Duke [Leopold of Baden](/wiki/Leopold,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Leopold, Grand Duke of Baden"), and their granddaughter [Victoria of Baden](/wiki/Victoria_of_Baden "Victoria of Baden") married the [Bernadotte](/wiki/House_of_Bernadotte "House of Bernadotte") king [Gustaf V of Sweden](/wiki/Gustaf_V_of_Sweden "Gustaf V of Sweden"). The present King [Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Carl XVI Gustaf") of Sweden is thus Gustav IV's heir through his grandfather, [Gustav VI Adolf](/wiki/Gustav_VI_Adolf "Gustav VI Adolf").

When King [Gustav V](/wiki/Gustav_V "Gustav V") publicly objected to the defence budget cuts made by Prime Minister [Karl Staaff](/wiki/Karl_Staaff "Karl Staaff") and the cabinet just before the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in the event known as the [Courtyard Crisis](/wiki/Courtyard_Crisis "Courtyard Crisis") accompanied by the [Peasant armament support march](/wiki/Peasant_armament_support_march "Peasant armament support march") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *bondetåget*), it was seen as a deliberate provocation by conservatives and reactionaries against the uncodified norm of a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") supported by the liberals and the social democrats, leading to Staaff's resignation.[[24]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:67-68-32) Gustaf V then appointed a caretaker government, supported by the conservatives, led by legal scholar [Hjalmar Hammarskjöld](/wiki/Hjalmar_Hammarskj%C3%B6ld "Hjalmar Hammarskjöld"), which remained in power longer than expected due to the outbreak of World War I (in which Sweden remained [neutral](/wiki/Swedish_neutrality "Swedish neutrality")). By then, increased defence spending was no longer a controversial issue.[[25]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:68-69-33) Nevertheless, in the year of the outbreak of the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution"), social tensions continued to rise; the general election in 1917 gave the liberals and social democrats greatly strengthened representation in both Riksdag chambers and a conservative government was no longer a defensible option.[[25]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:68-69-33) Following the definite breakthrough of [parliamentarism](/wiki/Parliamentarism "Parliamentarism") in 1917, with the appointment of the coalition government of liberals and social democrats led by professor [Nils Edén](/wiki/Nils_Ed%C3%A9n "Nils Edén"), the political influence of the King was considerably reduced and an unwritten constitutional precedent was set that would remain in effect until 1975.[[26]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:66-69-34)[[27]](#cite_note-Lewin:112-115-35)

Only during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), in the so-called [Midsummer crisis](/wiki/Midsummer_crisis "Midsummer crisis") (regarding the issue whether neutral Sweden should permit [rail transport of German troops from Norway passing through to Finland](/wiki/Transit_of_German_troops_through_Finland_and_Sweden "Transit of German troops through Finland and Sweden")), did Gustaf V allegedly try to intervene in the political process by threatening to [abdicate](/wiki/Abdication "Abdication").[[28]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:72-36)

King [Gustaf VI Adolf](/wiki/Gustaf_VI_Adolf "Gustaf VI Adolf") succeeded his elderly father who died in 1950, and he is generally regarded as a constitutional monarch who stayed out of politics and controversy. In 1954, a royal commission began work on whether Sweden should undergo constitutional reform to adapt the [1809 Instrument of Government](/wiki/1809_Instrument_of_Government "1809 Instrument of Government") to current political realities, or whether a new one should be written; ultimately the latter idea was chosen.[[29]](#cite_note-Bergman1999-37) The future role of the monarchy was settled in a manner well known within Swedish political discourse: a political compromise reached at the summer resort of [Torekov](/wiki/Torekov "Torekov") in 1971 (hence known as the *Torekov compromise*, [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Torekovskompromissen*) by representatives of four of the parties in the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") (the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Swedish_Social_Democratic_Party "Swedish Social Democratic Party"), the [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Sweden) "Centre Party (Sweden)"), the [Liberal People's Party](/wiki/Liberal_People%27s_Party_(Sweden) "Liberal People's Party (Sweden)"), and the [Moderate Party](/wiki/Moderate_Party "Moderate Party"), that is all the parties except the [Communists](/wiki/Left_Party_(Sweden) "Left Party (Sweden)")).[[n 9]](#cite_note-n6-39)[[29]](#cite_note-Bergman1999-37) It mandated that the monarchy would remain largely as it was but would become entirely ceremonial, without any residual political powers left.[[29]](#cite_note-Bergman1999-37)

Following the required double [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") votes that took place in 1973 and 1974, a new [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Constitution_of_Sweden "Constitution of Sweden") was brought into effect. The monarch's functions and duties, as defined in the 1974 Constitution Act, include heading the special cabinet council held when there is a change of government, but no executive powers with respect to the governance of the realm are vested in him.[[31]](#cite_note-Duties_of_the_Monarch-40)[[32]](#cite_note-The_Head_of_State-41)

[Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Carl XVI Gustaf") became king on 15 September 1973 on the death of his grandfather [Gustaf VI Adolf](/wiki/Gustaf_VI_Adolf "Gustaf VI Adolf") and because of his father's early death has become the longest reigning monarch in Swedish history.[[33]](#cite_note-SFS_1973:702-42) His [King's Golden Jubilee](/wiki/Golden_Jubilee_of_Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Golden Jubilee of Carl XVI Gustaf") was celebrated in 2023. Leading up to that year and including it, beginning already in 2018, some of the most serious criticism ever published took place about Carl Gustaf and the way the monarchy has developed during his reign.[[34]](#cite_note-43)[[8]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-8)[[9]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-9)

When, on 1 January 1975, it replaced the [Instrument of Government of 1809](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_of_1809 "Instrument of Government of 1809") as part of the [Constitution of Sweden](/wiki/Basic_Laws_of_Sweden "Basic Laws of Sweden"), the [Instrument of Government of 1974](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government_(1974) "Instrument of Government (1974)") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *1974 års regeringsform*) transformed the advisory [Council of State](/wiki/King_in_Council_(Sweden) "King in Council (Sweden)") (*Statsrådet*) into the collegial [Government](/wiki/Government_of_Sweden "Government of Sweden") (*Regeringen*), to which all executive power was transferred.[[35]](#cite_note-Nergelius:15-16-44)[[36]](#cite_note-Nergelius:33-34-45) Responsibility for nominating and dismissing the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Sweden "Prime Minister of Sweden") (who, since 1975, is elected by the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag")) was transferred to the [Speaker of the Riksdag](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Riksdag "Speaker of the Riksdag"); the prime minister appoints and dismisses the other [ministers](/wiki/Minister_(government) "Minister (government)") at his or her discretion.[[36]](#cite_note-Nergelius:33-34-45)[[37]](#cite_note-Forming_a_government-46)[[38]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:166-170-47)[[n 10]](#cite_note-n12-49) Furthermore, bills passed by the Riksdag become law without [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent"): the prime minister or any other cabinet minister signs them "On Behalf of the Government" (*På regeringens vägnar*).[[40]](#cite_note-Prop._1973:90-50)

Although the unwritten precedent was set in 1917, when [Gustaf V](/wiki/Gustaf_V "Gustaf V") had little choice but to support the idea of a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") and promised Prime Minister [Nils Edén](/wiki/Nils_Ed%C3%A9n "Nils Edén") to stop seeking advice from secret advisors other than the duly appointed cabinet ministers and not to interfere in politics again;[[27]](#cite_note-Lewin:112-115-35)[[41]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:65-69-51) the Torekov compromise, struck in 1971 by the four major parties at the time, provided, and continues to provide, a majority consensus in Swedish political discourse on the role of the monarchy within the constitutional framework.[[29]](#cite_note-Bergman1999-37)[[32]](#cite_note-The_Head_of_State-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Nergelius:41-52) The official motive for the radical changes which came to pass in 1975 was for it to be as descriptive as possible of the workings of the state and clear on how decisions actually are made.[[40]](#cite_note-Prop._1973:90-50) Minister of Justice [Lennart Geijer](/wiki/Lennart_Geijer "Lennart Geijer") further remarked on the 1973 government bill that any continued pretensions of royal involvement in government decision making would be of a "fictitious nature" and therefore "highly unsatisfactory".[[40]](#cite_note-Prop._1973:90-50)

Thus, the monarch lost all formal executive powers, becoming a ceremonial and representative [figurehead](/wiki/Figurehead "Figurehead").[[40]](#cite_note-Prop._1973:90-50)[[42]](#cite_note-Nergelius:41-52)[[43]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:166-53) The monarch, while explicitly referred to as the "Head of State" (*Statschefen*) in the 1974 Instrument of Government,[[n 11]](#cite_note-n26-55) is not even the [nominal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/nominal "wikt:nominal") chief executive.[[n 12]](#cite_note-n24-59)[[32]](#cite_note-The_Head_of_State-41)[[35]](#cite_note-Nergelius:15-16-44)[[48]](#cite_note-Petersson:44-60) The Instrument of Government of 1974 does grant the person serving as king or queen regnant [absolute immunity](/wiki/Absolute_immunity "Absolute immunity") from criminal (but not civil) charges for as long as he or she remains in office.[[49]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-61)[[n 13]](#cite_note-n14-62) The monarch therefore cannot be prosecuted or otherwise held to account for his or her actions, both official and private, in judicial proceedings.[[49]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-61) None of the other members of the [royal family](/wiki/Swedish_Royal_Family "Swedish Royal Family") or the employees the [Royal Court](/wiki/Royal_Court_of_Sweden "Royal Court of Sweden") enjoy similar immunity.[[49]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-61)

At the request of the Speaker of the Riksdag, the monarch [opens the annual session of the Riksdag](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Riksdag "Opening of the Riksdag") (*Riksmötets öppnande*) in the chamber of the [Riksdag building](/wiki/Riksdag_building "Riksdag building").[[n 14]](#cite_note-n25-64)[[51]](#cite_note-A_year_in_the_Riksdag-65) The king or [queen regnant](/wiki/Queen_regnant "Queen regnant") also receives [Letters of Credence](/wiki/Letters_of_Credence "Letters of Credence") of foreign ambassadors sent to Sweden and signs those of Swedish ambassadors sent abroad.[[31]](#cite_note-Duties_of_the_Monarch-40) The monarch also chairs the [Cabinet Council](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Sweden) "Council of State (Sweden)") (*skifteskonselj*) in a session that establishes the new government following a [general election](/wiki/Elections_in_Sweden "Elections in Sweden") or major cabinet reshuffle and also chairs information councils (*informationskonselj*) approximately four times a year to get information from the assembled Government, apart from that given by ministers in individual audiences or through other means.[[31]](#cite_note-Duties_of_the_Monarch-40)[[52]](#cite_note-Nergelius:41-42-66) Formally, it is the explicit responsibility of the prime minister to keep the monarch informed on the affairs of the realm; the failure to do so following the [2004 tsunami disaster in the Indian Ocean](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami") (in which many Swedes perished) gave rise to wide criticism of Prime Minister [Göran Persson](/wiki/G%C3%B6ran_Persson "Göran Persson") for his handling of the matter.[[49]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-61) The monarch also chairs the Advisory Council on Foreign Affairs (*Utrikesnämnden*), a body that enables the government of the day to inform not only the head of state, but also the speaker and representatives of the opposition parties in the Riksdag, on foreign affairs issues in a confidential manner.[[31]](#cite_note-Duties_of_the_Monarch-40)[[52]](#cite_note-Nergelius:41-42-66)[[53]](#cite_note-The_Advisory_Council_on_Foreign_Affairs-67)

While the monarch is no longer the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief#Sweden "Commander-in-chief") (*högste befälhavare*) of the [Swedish Armed Forces](/wiki/Swedish_Armed_Forces "Swedish Armed Forces"), as he once was under the 1809 Instrument of Government,[[29]](#cite_note-Bergman1999-37) he is the foremost representative of the Swedish defence establishment and holds supreme rank in each of the service arms. He ranks as a four star [admiral](/wiki/Admiral "Admiral") in the [Swedish Navy](/wiki/Swedish_Navy "Swedish Navy") and general in the [Swedish Army](/wiki/Swedish_Army "Swedish Army") and [Air Force](/wiki/Swedish_Air_Force "Swedish Air Force").[[31]](#cite_note-Duties_of_the_Monarch-40) As part of his court, the monarch has a [military staff](/wiki/Staff_(military) "Staff (military)"), which is headed by a senior officer (usually a general or admiral, retired from active service) and includes active duty military officers serving as [aides-de-camp](/wiki/Aide-de-camp "Aide-de-camp") to the monarch and his or her family.[[54]](#cite_note-Övriga_funktioner-68)

## Cultural role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cultural role")]

The monarch and members of the [Royal Family](/wiki/Swedish_Royal_Family "Swedish Royal Family") undertake a variety of official, unofficial and other representative duties within Sweden and abroad. The monarch and his or her family play a central role in [state visits](/wiki/State_visit "State visit") to Sweden and conduct state visits to other nations on behalf of Sweden. Other members of the Royal Family may also represent the country abroad at lesser functions.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Royal_standard_of_Sweden.svg/250px-Royal_standard_of_Sweden.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_standard_of_Sweden.svg)

The royal standard used by the monarch

Many of the Swedish [general flag flying days](/wiki/Flag_flying_days_in_Sweden "Flag flying days in Sweden") have direct royal connections; among them are the [name days](/wiki/Name_days_in_Sweden "Name days in Sweden") of the King (28 January), the Queen (8 August), and the Crown Princess (12 March); the birthdays of the King (30 April), the Queen (23 December), and the Crown Princess (14 July); and [Gustavus Adolphus Day](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus_Day "Gustavus Adolphus Day") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Gustav Adolfsdagen*), on 6 November, in memory of King [Gustavus Adolphus](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus_of_Sweden "Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden"), who was killed on that date ([old style](/wiki/Adoption_of_the_Gregorian_calendar#Sweden "Adoption of the Gregorian calendar")) in 1632 in the [Battle of Lützen](/wiki/Battle_of_L%C3%BCtzen_(1632) "Battle of Lützen (1632)").[[n 15]](#cite_note-n4-69)[[55]](#cite_note-70)[[56]](#cite_note-71)[[57]](#cite_note-72) None of these flag days are [public holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Sweden "Public holidays in Sweden"), however.[[n 16]](#cite_note-n21-74)

Perhaps the most globally known ceremony in which the Royal Family annually participate is the Nobel Prize award ceremony held at the [Stockholm Concert Hall](/wiki/Stockholm_Concert_Hall "Stockholm Concert Hall") (and the subsequent [banquet](/wiki/Nobel_Banquet "Nobel Banquet") in the [Stockholm City Hall](/wiki/Stockholm_City_Hall "Stockholm City Hall")), where the monarch hands out the Nobel Prizes on behalf of the [Nobel Foundation](/wiki/Nobel_Foundation "Nobel Foundation") for outstanding contributions to mankind in [physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics"), [chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), [literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"), [physiology or medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine"), and the [economic sciences](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences").[[59]](#cite_note-Levinovitz21-23-75)

*[Eriksgata](/wiki/Eriksgata "Eriksgata")* was the name of the traditional journey of newly elected medieval [Swedish kings](/wiki/List_of_Swedish_monarchs "List of Swedish monarchs") through important [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Sweden "Provinces of Sweden") to have their election confirmed by local *[Things](/wiki/Thing_(assembly) "Thing (assembly)")*. The actual election took place at the [Stone of Mora](/wiki/Stone_of_Mora "Stone of Mora") in [Uppland](/wiki/Uppland "Uppland") and participation was originally restricted to the people of that area; hence, the need of having the election confirmed by the other parts of the realm. The Eriksgata gradually lost its importance when, as of the 14th century, representatives of other parts of Sweden began to participate in the election. After 1544, when [hereditary monarchy](/wiki/Hereditary_monarchy "Hereditary monarchy") was instituted, that meant that the Eriksgata had little practical importance. The last king to travel the Eriksgata according to the old tradition was [Charles IX](/wiki/Charles_IX_of_Sweden "Charles IX of Sweden"), whose reign began in 1604. Later, kings, up until present times, have made visits to all the Swedish provinces and called them an *Eriksgata*, while those visits bear little resemblance to the medieval tradition.

## Titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Titles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Throne_of_Sweden_1982.jpg/250px-Throne_of_Sweden_1982.jpg)](/wiki/File:Throne_of_Sweden_1982.jpg)

The [Silver Throne](/wiki/Silver_Throne "Silver Throne"), used by all Swedish monarchs from [Queen Christina](/wiki/Christina,_Queen_of_Sweden "Christina, Queen of Sweden") in 1650 onward

### Monarch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Monarch")]

The full title of the Swedish monarch from 1523[[60]](#cite_note-76)[[61]](#cite_note-77) to 1973 was:

:   In [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Med Guds Nåde Sveriges, Götes och Vendes Konung*
:   In [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Dei Gratia Suecorum, Gothorum et Vandalorum Rex*

Translated as "[By the Grace of God](/wiki/By_the_Grace_of_God "By the Grace of God"), King of the Swedes, [the Goths](/wiki/King_of_the_Goths "King of the Goths"), and [the Wends](/wiki/King_of_the_Wends "King of the Wends")"

During the reign of the [House of Holstein-Gottorp](/wiki/House_of_Holstein-Gottorp_(Swedish_line) "House of Holstein-Gottorp (Swedish line)") from 1751 to 1818, the title *Heir to Norway* (*Arvinge till Norge*) was also used,[[62]](#cite_note-AOS:P-78) as well as other titles connected to the [Dukes of Holstein-Gottorp](/wiki/Duke_of_Holstein-Gottorp "Duke of Holstein-Gottorp"). When, after the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), Norway was in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with Sweden, the title included *King of Norway*, in older Swedish spellings: *Sweriges, Norriges, Göthes och Wendes Konung*.

Upon his accession, [Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Carl XVI Gustaf") chose for his title simply *Sveriges Konung* (King of Sweden).[[33]](#cite_note-SFS_1973:702-42)

### Dynasts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Dynasts")]

The customary title of the [heir apparent](/wiki/Heir_apparent "Heir apparent") is [crown prince](/wiki/Crown_prince "Crown prince") (*kronprins*) or crown princess (*kronprinsessa*). The wife of a crown prince would also receive a corresponding title, but not the husband of a crown princess. The traditional official title used until 1980 for other dynastic male heirs was hereditary prince (*[arvfurste](/wiki/Arvfurste "Arvfurste")*), although the word prince (*prins*) was used in constitutional legal texts such as the [Act of Succession](/wiki/Act_of_Succession_(1810) "Act of Succession (1810)") and also colloquially and informally. Female dynasts are titled princess (*prinsessa*).

The Swedish Succession Act was altered in 1980 to allow for female succession to the throne.[[63]](#cite_note-79)

### Ducal titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ducal titles")]

Main article: [Duchies in Sweden](/wiki/Duchies_in_Sweden "Duchies in Sweden")

King [Gustav III](/wiki/Gustav_III "Gustav III") revived a tradition from the time of [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_Vasa "Gustav Vasa") and the medieval era by giving male heirs to the throne [ducal titles](/wiki/Duchies_in_Sweden "Duchies in Sweden") of [Swedish provinces](/wiki/Swedish_provinces "Swedish provinces"). The difference between the ducal titles from the Vasa era and those granted by Gustav III is they now are non-hereditary courtesy titles given at birth. Since 1980, they have been conferred to all royal heirs, male and female. The wives of royal dukes have always shared their husbands' titles; the husbands of royal duchesses have done so as of 2010.

## Symbols of the monarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Symbols of the monarchy")]

### Regalia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Regalia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Royal_crown_of_Sweden.jpg/250px-Royal_crown_of_Sweden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_crown_of_Sweden.jpg)

The [Crown of Eric XIV](/wiki/Crown_of_Eric_XIV "Crown of Eric XIV")

The [regalia of Sweden](/wiki/Regalia_of_Sweden "Regalia of Sweden") are kept deep in the vaults of the [Treasury chamber](/w/index.php?title=Treasury_of_Sweden&action=edit&redlink=1 "Treasury of Sweden (page does not exist)") [[sv](https://sv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skattkammaren "sv:Skattkammaren")] ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Skattkammaren*), located underneath the [Royal Palace in Stockholm](/wiki/Royal_Palace_in_Stockholm "Royal Palace in Stockholm"), in a museum which has been open to the public since 1970. Among the oldest objects in the collection are the sword of [Gustav Vasa](/wiki/Gustav_Vasa "Gustav Vasa") and the crown, orb, sceptre and key of [King Erik XIV](/wiki/King_Erik_XIV "King Erik XIV"). The Regalia is state property and the government authority which holds it in trust is the [Legal, Financial and Administrative Services Agency](/wiki/Legal,_Financial_and_Administrative_Services_Agency "Legal, Financial and Administrative Services Agency").[[64]](#cite_note-History,_The_Treasury-80)[[65]](#cite_note-Regal_symbols-81)

The last king to have been crowned was [Oscar II](/wiki/Oscar_II "Oscar II"). His son and successor, [Gustaf V](/wiki/Gustaf_V "Gustaf V"), abstained from having a coronation.[[64]](#cite_note-History,_The_Treasury-80) While the crowns and coronets have not been worn by Swedish royalty since 1907, they are nevertheless still displayed on royal occasions such as at weddings, [christenings](/wiki/Swedish_royal_baptisms "Swedish royal baptisms") and funerals. Until 1974, the crown and sceptre were also displayed on cushions beside the [Silver Throne](/wiki/Silver_Throne "Silver Throne") at the annual solemn opening of the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Riksdagens högtidliga öppnande*).[[64]](#cite_note-History,_The_Treasury-80)[[65]](#cite_note-Regal_symbols-81)[[66]](#cite_note-82)

### Royal orders of chivalry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Royal orders of chivalry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Sv_Ordenstkn_3.jpg/250px-Sv_Ordenstkn_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sv_Ordenstkn_3.jpg)

The Royal Orders of Sweden constituting the Royal Order of Knights

The Royal orders have a historical basis, dating back to the 1606 founding of the now extinct [Jehova Order](/wiki/Jehova_Order "Jehova Order"). The Royal Orders of Knights of Sweden were only truly codified in the 18th century, with their formal foundation in 1748 by King [Frederick I](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Sweden "Frederick I of Sweden"). In 1974 the Riksdag significantly changed the conditions and criteria under which orders and decorations could be awarded: that no Swedish citizen outside the Royal Family is eligible to receive such decorations. The [Order of the Seraphim](/wiki/Order_of_the_Seraphim "Order of the Seraphim") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Serafimerorden*) is only awarded to foreign [heads of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and members of the Swedish and foreign royal families, while the [Order of the Polar Star](/wiki/Order_of_the_Polar_Star "Order of the Polar Star") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Nordstjärneorden*) was only to be bestowed on any non-Swedish citizen.[[67]](#cite_note-83) Following the reforms, the [Order of the Sword](/wiki/Order_of_the_Sword "Order of the Sword") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Svärdsorden*) and the [Order of Vasa](/wiki/Order_of_Vasa "Order of Vasa") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Vasaorden*) were no longer conferred: officially they were declared as "dormant". In 2022 the government proposed a new reform taking effect in 2023 that once again opened the Royal Orders so that they now may be awarded to Swedish citizens again. The Order of the Seraphim however was not affected by these changes.

Between 1975 and 2023, [H. M. The King's Medal](/wiki/H._M._The_King%27s_Medal "H. M. The King's Medal") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *H.M. Konungens medalj*) was the highest honour that could be awarded to Swedish citizens other than members of the [Royal Family](/wiki/Swedish_Royal_Family "Swedish Royal Family").

### Royal residences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Royal residences")]

Main article: [Crown palaces in Sweden](/wiki/Crown_palaces_in_Sweden "Crown palaces in Sweden")

The Royal Palaces (including the [Royal Palace in Stockholm](/wiki/Royal_Palace_in_Stockholm "Royal Palace in Stockholm"), [Drottningholm Palace](/wiki/Drottningholm_Palace "Drottningholm Palace"), [Haga Palace](/wiki/Haga_Palace "Haga Palace"), [Rosendal Palace](/wiki/Rosendal_Palace "Rosendal Palace"), [Ulriksdal Palace](/wiki/Ulriksdal_Palace "Ulriksdal Palace"), [Rosersberg Palace](/wiki/Rosersberg_Palace "Rosersberg Palace"), [Tullgarn Palace](/wiki/Tullgarn_Palace "Tullgarn Palace") and [Gripsholm Castle](/wiki/Gripsholm_Castle "Gripsholm Castle")) are government property, managed by the [National Property Board](/wiki/National_Property_Board_of_Sweden "National Property Board of Sweden") ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Statens fastighetsverk*) and are at the disposal of the Monarch, an arrangement that has been in place since the beginning of the 19th century.[[68]](#cite_note-Svenska_folkets_slott-84)[[69]](#cite_note-State_administration_in_Sweden-85) There are also residences which are held privately by the [Royal Family](/wiki/Swedish_Royal_Family "Swedish Royal Family"), such as [Solliden Palace](/wiki/Solliden_Palace "Solliden Palace") on the island of [Öland](/wiki/%C3%96land "Öland"), a cottage in [Storlien](/wiki/Storlien "Storlien") in the [Jämtland](/wiki/J%C3%A4mtland "Jämtland") and Villa Mirage in [Sainte-Maxime](/wiki/Sainte-Maxime "Sainte-Maxime") in southern France (originally acquired by [Prince Bertil](/wiki/Prince_Bertil,_Duke_of_Halland "Prince Bertil, Duke of Halland")).[[70]](#cite_note-Interests-86)

#### Royal Palace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Royal Palace")]

Main article: [Stockholm Palace](/wiki/Stockholm_Palace "Stockholm Palace")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Kunliga_slottet_2_copy1.jpg/250px-Kunliga_slottet_2_copy1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kunliga_slottet_2_copy1.jpg)

The [Royal Palace](/wiki/Stockholm_Palace "Stockholm Palace") in Stockholm, as seen from the tower of the [Cathedral](/wiki/Stockholm_Cathedral "Stockholm Cathedral")

The **Royal Palace** (*Kungliga slottet*), also known as **Stockholm Palace** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Stockholms slott*), is the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") of the king. The Royal Palace is located on *[Stadsholmen](/wiki/Stadsholmen "Stadsholmen")* ("City Island"), commonly known as *[Gamla Stan](/wiki/Gamla_Stan "Gamla Stan")* ("the Old Town") in the national capital city [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm").

The offices of the king, other members of the [Swedish Royal Family](/wiki/Swedish_Royal_Family "Swedish Royal Family"), and the offices of the [Royal Court](/wiki/Royal_Court_of_Sweden "Royal Court of Sweden") are located in the palace. The Royal Palace is used for representative purposes and State occasions by the king.[[71]](#cite_note-The_Royal_Palace_of_Stockholm-87) The Royal Palace is guarded by *[Högvakten](/wiki/Royal_Guards_(Sweden) "Royal Guards (Sweden)")*, a [royal guard](/wiki/Royal_guard "Royal guard"), consisting of regular service members of the [Swedish Armed Forces](/wiki/Swedish_Armed_Forces "Swedish Armed Forces").[[72]](#cite_note-About_the_Royal_Guards-88) The tradition of having a regular unit of the [Army](/wiki/Swedish_Army "Swedish Army") guarding at the royal residence dates back to 1523.[[72]](#cite_note-About_the_Royal_Guards-88) Until the mid-19th century, the royal guards also maintained law and order in the city and provided firefighting services.[[72]](#cite_note-About_the_Royal_Guards-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Slottet_Tre_Kronor_1661.jpg/250px-Slottet_Tre_Kronor_1661.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slottet_Tre_Kronor_1661.jpg)

The castle *[Tre Kronor](/wiki/Tre_Kronor_(castle) "Tre Kronor (castle)")*, located on the site of today's palace, in a painting from 1661 by [Govert Dircksz Camphuysen](/wiki/Govert_Dircksz_Camphuysen "Govert Dircksz Camphuysen")

The southern façade faces the grand style slope *[Slottsbacken](/wiki/Slottsbacken "Slottsbacken")*;[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] the eastern façade borders *[Skeppsbron](/wiki/Skeppsbron "Skeppsbron")*, a quay which passes along the eastern waterfront of the old town; on the northern front *[Lejonbacken](/wiki/Lejonbacken "Lejonbacken")* is a system of ramps named for the [Medici lions](/wiki/Medici_lions "Medici lions"), sculptures on the stone railings; and the western wings border the open space *[Högvaktsterrassen](/wiki/H%C3%B6gvaktsterrassen "Högvaktsterrassen")*. The Royal Palace in Stockholm is unique among European royal residences in that large portions of it are open year-round to visitors, who pay entrance fees.[[71]](#cite_note-The_Royal_Palace_of_Stockholm-87)

The first building on this site was a [fortress](/wiki/Fortress "Fortress") with a [keep](/wiki/Keep "Keep") built in the 13th century by [Birger Jarl](/wiki/Birger_Jarl "Birger Jarl") to defend the entry into [Lake Mälaren](/wiki/Lake_M%C3%A4laren "Lake Mälaren"). The fortress gradually grew to a castle, known as *[Tre Kronor](/wiki/Tre_kronor_(castle) "Tre kronor (castle)")*: named after the spire on the centre tower with [Three Crowns](/wiki/Three_Crowns "Three Crowns"), which have become the Swedish national symbol.[[n 17]](#cite_note-n88-89) In the late 16th century, work was done to transform the castle into a [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") palace during the reign of [John III](/wiki/John_III_of_Sweden "John III of Sweden"). In 1690, it was decided that the castle be rebuilt in [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") style in a design by [Nicodemus Tessin the Younger](/wiki/Nicodemus_Tessin_the_Younger "Nicodemus Tessin the Younger"). In 1692, work began on the northern row. However, much of the old castle was destroyed in a disastrous fire on 7 May 1697.

Tessin rebuilt the damaged [palace](/wiki/Palace "Palace"), and work continued for another 63 years. Semicircular wings around the outer western [courtyard](/wiki/Courtyard "Courtyard") were finished in 1734, the palace church was finished in the 1740s, and the exterior was finished in 1754. The royal family moved to the palace with the southwest, southeast, and northeast wings finished. The northwest wing was finished in 1760. In the north, *Lejonbacken* (the "Lion's Slope") was rebuilt from 1824 to 1830.

#### Drottningholm Palace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Drottningholm Palace")]

Main article: [Drottningholm Palace](/wiki/Drottningholm_Palace "Drottningholm Palace")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Drottningholm_Palace_viewed_from_east_2005-08-14.jpg/250px-Drottningholm_Palace_viewed_from_east_2005-08-14.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drottningholm_Palace_viewed_from_east_2005-08-14.jpg)

Drottningholm Palace, a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), is the home residence of the King and Queen.[[73]](#cite_note-Drottningholm_Palace-90)

**Drottningholm Palace** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Drottningholms slott*) is located at [Drottningholm](/wiki/Drottningholm "Drottningholm") on the island of [Lovön](/wiki/Lov%C3%B6n "Lovön") (in [Ekerö Municipality](/wiki/Eker%C3%B6_Municipality "Ekerö Municipality") of [Stockholm County](/wiki/Stockholm_County "Stockholm County")), and is one of [Sweden's Royal Palaces](/wiki/Royal_Palaces_in_Sweden "Royal Palaces in Sweden"). It was originally built in the late 16th century. It has served as a residence of the Swedish royal family members for most of the 18th and 19th centuries. Apart from being the current private residence of the King and Queen, Drottningholm Palace is a popular tourist attraction.[[73]](#cite_note-Drottningholm_Palace-90)

The gardens and park areas surrounding Drottningholm Palace and adjacent to its buildings are one of the main attractions for the tourists that visit the palace each year. The gardens have been established in stages since the palace was first built, resulting in many different styles.[[74]](#cite_note-Drottningholm_Palace_Park-91)

The royal domain of Drottningholm is a well-preserved milieu from the 17th and 18th centuries, inspired by French buildings such as the [Chateau of Versailles](/wiki/Chateau_of_Versailles "Chateau of Versailles"), and is a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), principally because of the [Drottningholm Palace Theatre](/wiki/Drottningholm_Palace_Theatre "Drottningholm Palace Theatre") and the [Chinese Pavilion at Drottningholm](/wiki/Chinese_Pavilion_at_Drottningholm "Chinese Pavilion at Drottningholm"). It was added to the [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List") in 1991.[[75]](#cite_note-The_World_Heritage-92)

#### Haga Palace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Haga Palace")]

Main article: [Haga Palace](/wiki/Haga_Palace "Haga Palace")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Haga_slott_2008b.jpg/250px-Haga_slott_2008b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haga_slott_2008b.jpg)

Haga Palace is the residence of [Crown Princess Victoria](/wiki/Crown_Princess_Victoria "Crown Princess Victoria") and her family.

**Haga Palace** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Haga slott*), formerly known as the **Queen's Pavilion** ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Drottningens paviljong*), is located in the [Haga Park](/wiki/Hagaparken "Hagaparken"), [Solna Municipality](/wiki/Solna_Municipality "Solna Municipality") in [Metropolitan Stockholm](/wiki/Metropolitan_Stockholm "Metropolitan Stockholm"). The palace, built between 1802 – 1805, was modelled after balletmaster [Louis Gallodier](/wiki/Louis_Gallodier "Louis Gallodier")'s Italian villa at [Drottningholm](/wiki/Drottningholm "Drottningholm") by architect [Carl Christoffer Gjörwell](/wiki/Carl_Christoffer_Gj%C3%B6rwell "Carl Christoffer Gjörwell") on appointment by [King Gustaf IV Adolf](/wiki/King_Gustaf_IV_Adolf "King Gustaf IV Adolf") for the royal children. It has been the home or summer house of several members of the Swedish royal family – notably it was the birthplace of the present King [Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf "Carl XVI Gustaf") – until 1966 when [King Gustaf VI Adolf](/wiki/King_Gustaf_VI_Adolf "King Gustaf VI Adolf") transferred its disposal to the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Sweden "Prime Minister of Sweden") and it was turned into a guest house for distinguished foreign official visitors ([heads of state](/wiki/Heads_of_state "Heads of state") and [heads of government](/wiki/Heads_of_government "Heads of government") et cetera).[[76]](#cite_note-Buildings_in_Haga_Park-93)

In April 2009, it was announced by Prime Minister [Fredrik Reinfeldt](/wiki/Fredrik_Reinfeldt "Fredrik Reinfeldt") that the rights of disposal of the palace would be transferred back to the King and thus could be used by [Crown Princess Victoria](/wiki/Crown_Princess_Victoria "Crown Princess Victoria") and her husband, [Prince Daniel, Duke of Västergötland](/wiki/Prince_Daniel,_Duke_of_V%C3%A4sterg%C3%B6tland "Prince Daniel, Duke of Västergötland").[[77]](#cite_note-Regeringen_återlämnar_Haga_slott-94) They moved into the palace in the autumn after their [wedding on 19 June 2010](/wiki/Wedding_of_Victoria,_Crown_Princess_of_Sweden,_and_Daniel_Westling "Wedding of Victoria, Crown Princess of Sweden, and Daniel Westling").[[78]](#cite_note-Haga_Palace-95)

Main article: [Swedish royal family](/wiki/Swedish_royal_family "Swedish royal family")

The Swedish royal family is, according to the [Royal Court](/wiki/Royal_Court_of_Sweden "Royal Court of Sweden"), currently categorized into three groups;

* first, those with royal titles and [style (manner of address)](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)") who perform official and unofficial engagements for the nation, are the members of the Royal Family ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Kungafamiljen*) (currently this category only includes the King, Queen and their descendants, including spouses);[[79]](#cite_note-Möt_Kungafamiljen-96)
* second, those with royal titles and style (manner of address) who perform no official engagements ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Kungliga Huset*, usually stylized with the shortform *Kungl. Huset*);[[79]](#cite_note-Möt_Kungafamiljen-96)
* and third, the extended family of the King ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Kungliga Familjens övriga medlemmar*, usually stylized with the shortform *Kungl. Familjens övriga medlemmar*) which is other close relatives who are not dynasts and thus do not represent the country officially.[[79]](#cite_note-Möt_Kungafamiljen-96)

However, in any case, there is no legislation or other public document which delineates the rules of membership in either the Royal House or Royal Family, as it is left to the sole discretion of the King.

### The line of succession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: The line of succession")]

Main article: [Succession to the Swedish throne](/wiki/Succession_to_the_Swedish_throne "Succession to the Swedish throne")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Vasaorden_3.JPG/250px-Vasaorden_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vasaorden_3.JPG)

The royal barge *Vasaorden*, last used at the [2010 royal wedding](/wiki/Wedding_of_Victoria,_Crown_Princess_of_Sweden,_and_Daniel_Westling "Wedding of Victoria, Crown Princess of Sweden, and Daniel Westling")

The [Act of Succession of 1810](/wiki/Swedish_Act_of_Succession "Swedish Act of Succession") provides the rules governing the [line of succession](/wiki/Line_of_succession_to_the_Swedish_throne "Line of succession to the Swedish throne") and designates the legitimate heirs to the Swedish Throne; it also states in [article 4](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Act_of_Succession_of_Sweden "wikisource:Act of Succession of Sweden") that the Monarch and dynastic members of the Royal House must at all times be a [Protestant Christian](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") of the *pure evangelical faith* (by implication the Evangelical-Lutheran [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden")).[[80]](#cite_note-The_Act_of_Succession-97)[[81]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-44-98)

A rewrite of the Act, entering into force in 1980, fundamentally changed the rules of succession from [agnatic primogeniture](/wiki/Agnatic_primogeniture "Agnatic primogeniture") to [absolute primogeniture](/wiki/Absolute_primogeniture "Absolute primogeniture").[[81]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-44-98) This allowed for the crown to pass to the eldest child regardless of gender and thus [retroactively](/wiki/Ex_post_facto_law "Ex post facto law") installed Princess Victoria as crown princess over her younger brother, [Prince Carl Philip](/wiki/Prince_Carl_Philip "Prince Carl Philip"), who had been born as crown prince a few months before.

In its present reading, Article 1 of the Act of Succession limits the potential number of claimants to the throne, so that only the descendants of Carl XVI Gustaf can inherit the Throne.[[81]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-44-98)[[82]](#cite_note-AOS:1-99) If the royal house were to be extinct, the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") is not obligated to elect a new royal house, as it once was up until the constitutional reforms of the 1970s.[[49]](#cite_note-Nergelius:42-61)
