## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monarchy_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-n2_14-0)** A complete Lutheran church ordinance was not presented until the [Swedish Church Ordinance 1571](/wiki/Swedish_Church_Ordinance_1571 "Swedish Church Ordinance 1571"), with a [statement of faith](/wiki/Statement_of_faith "Statement of faith") finalized by the [Uppsala Synod](/wiki/Uppsala_Synod "Uppsala Synod") in 1593.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-n3_15-0)** The powers of the king were originally regulated by a section of the written legal code called *Konungabalk* (*Kings' partition*) from medieval times until 1734, when [a new law code](/wiki/Civil_Code_of_1734 "Civil Code of 1734") of Sweden was adopted and that section was removed. The new law code of Sweden was adopted after a long period of inquiries by [royal commissions](/wiki/Royal_commission "Royal commission") since the days of [Charles IX](/wiki/Charles_IX_of_Sweden "Charles IX of Sweden") (late 16th/early 17th century)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-n20_18-0)** No [regnal number](/wiki/Regnal_number "Regnal number") – just *Sigismund* – is used when referring to [Sigismund III Vasa](/wiki/Sigismund_III_Vasa "Sigismund III Vasa") as King of Sweden.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-n1_19-0)** The war against Denmark was concluded in 1613 with a peace treaty, which did not cost Sweden any territory, but Sweden was nevertheless forced to pay a heavy indemnity to Denmark ([Treaty of Knäred](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kn%C3%A4red "Treaty of Knäred")) in order to regain control of [Älvsborg Fortress](/wiki/%C3%84lvsborg_Fortress "Älvsborg Fortress").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-n17_23-0)** Meanwhile, a [Catholic army](/wiki/Catholic_League_(German) "Catholic League (German)") under Tilly was laying waste to [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"). Gustavus Adolphus met Tilly's army and crushed it at the [First Battle of Breitenfeld](/wiki/Battle_of_Breitenfeld_(1631) "Battle of Breitenfeld (1631)") in September 1631. He then marched clear across Germany, establishing his winter quarters near the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"), making plans for the invasion of the rest of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). In March 1632, Gustavus Adolphus invaded [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), a staunch ally of the [Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"). He forced the withdrawal of his Catholic opponents at the [Battle of Rain](/wiki/Battle_of_Rain "Battle of Rain"). In the summer of that year, he sought a political solution that would preserve the existing structure of states in Germany, while guaranteeing the security of its Protestants. But achieving these objectives depended on his continued success on the battlefield.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-n8_24-0)** In Chapter V of [Carl von Clausewitz](/wiki/Carl_von_Clausewitz "Carl von Clausewitz")' *[On War](/wiki/On_War "On War")*, he lists Gustavus Adolphus as an example of an outstanding military leader, along with: [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great"), [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar"), [Alexander Farnese](/wiki/Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma"), [Charles XII](/wiki/Charles_XII "Charles XII"), [Frederick the Great](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great") and [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") Bonaparte.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-n10_25-0)** Although the local conflict with Denmark-Norway, as part of the Thirty Years' War was settled at the Second Treaty of Brömsebro (1645), in which the Danes ceded the Norwegian provinces of [Jämtland](/wiki/J%C3%A4mtland "Jämtland"), [Härjedalen](/wiki/H%C3%A4rjedalen "Härjedalen") and [Idre & Särna](/wiki/%C3%84lvdalen_Municipality "Älvdalen Municipality") as well as the Danish [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") islands of [Gotland](/wiki/Gotland "Gotland") and [Ösel](/wiki/Saaremaa "Saaremaa"). Sweden was furthermore exempted from the [Sound Dues](/wiki/Sound_Dues "Sound Dues") and received the Danish province of [Halland](/wiki/Halland "Halland") for a period of 30 years as a guarantee of these provisions.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-n18_26-0)** Sweden ceded its Baltic provinces and parts of Finland to Russia in the 1721 [Treaty of Nystad](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nystad "Treaty of Nystad").
9. **[^](#cite_ref-n6_39-0)** Also known as the Torekov Agreement ([Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *Torekovsövernskommelsen*). The participants were Valter Åman (s), [Bertil Fiskesjö](/wiki/Bertil_Fiskesj%C3%B6 "Bertil Fiskesjö") (c), Birger Lundström (fp) and Allan Hernelius (m).[[30]](#cite_note-38)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-n12_49-0)** The Speaker of the Riksdag, not the Prime Minister, is considered the second highest public office in the [order of precedence](/wiki/Swedish_order_of_precedence "Swedish order of precedence"), below the head of state.[[39]](#cite_note-Larsson_&_Bäck:155-48)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-n26_55-0)** Such as in the first article in which the monarch is mentioned:
    > **Art. 5.** The King or Queen who occupies the throne of Sweden in accordance with the [Act of Succession](/wiki/Swedish_Act_of_Succession "Swedish Act of Succession") shall be the Head of State.[[44]](#cite_note-IOG_1:5-54)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-n24_59-0)** Given their predominance in 20th century Swedish politics, the public positions taken by the leaders of the Social Democrats are noteworthy; particularly given that their party programme does call for the abolishment of the monarchy.[[45]](#cite_note-Socialdemokraterna_och_republikfrågan-56) Party leaders and prime ministers [Hjalmar Branting](/wiki/Hjalmar_Branting "Hjalmar Branting"), [Per-Albin Hansson](/wiki/Per-Albin_Hansson "Per-Albin Hansson") and [Tage Erlander](/wiki/Tage_Erlander "Tage Erlander") all made statements to the effect of being for a republic in principle whenever the issue was raised, but that it was not worth pursuing (presumably fearing an electoral backlash).[[46]](#cite_note-Åse:58-60-57)
    At the 1972 party congress of the Social Democrats, Prime Minister [Olof Palme](/wiki/Olof_Palme "Olof Palme") publicly defended the Torekov compromise, in response some members that yearned for a republic, by famously characterizing that the upcoming reforms would reduce the constitutional role of the monarchy to nothing but a "[plume](/wiki/Plume_(feather) "Plume (feather)")" (*plym*) and thus paving the way to abolish the monarchy with the *stroke of a pen* (*penndrag*) at some distant point in the future. Palme emphasized though that other reforms were far more important for the Social Democrats than abolishing the monarchy.[[45]](#cite_note-Socialdemokraterna_och_republikfrågan-56)[[47]](#cite_note-Åse:11-13-58)
    Successive leaders (and prime ministers) [Ingvar Carlsson](/wiki/Ingvar_Carlsson "Ingvar Carlsson") and Göran Persson have also defended the status quo.[[45]](#cite_note-Socialdemokraterna_och_republikfrågan-56)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-n14_62-0)** This could be interpreted as "for life", given the historical precedent: no voluntary abdication has occurred since [Ulrika Eleonora](/wiki/Ulrika_Eleonora "Ulrika Eleonora"), in 1719 and only three hereditary monarchs have been involuntarily deposed ([Eric XIV](/wiki/Eric_XIV "Eric XIV") in 1568, [Sigisumnd](/wiki/Sigismund_III_Vasa "Sigismund III Vasa") 1599, and [Gustav IV Adolf](/wiki/Gustav_IV_Adolf "Gustav IV Adolf") in 1809).
14. **[^](#cite_ref-n25_64-0)** The Riksdag Act provision in question reads:
    > **Special meeting for the opening of the Riksdag session**
    >
    > **Art. 6.** A special meeting of the Chamber for the formal opening of a Riksdag session takes
    > place no later than the third day of the session. At the request of the Speaker, the Head of State
    > declares the session open. If the Head of State is unable to attend, the Speaker declares the session
    > open.  
    > At this meeting, the Prime Minister delivers a statement of Government policy unless there are special grounds why he or she should refrain from doing so.
    >   
    > **Time of meeting for the opening of the Riksdag session**  
    > *Supplementary provision* **3.6.1** The formal opening of the session after an election to the Riksdag takes place at 2 p.m. on the second day of the session.  
    > In years in which no election to the Riksdag has been held, the formal opening takes place on the first day of the session at the same time.
    >
    > The Speaker may appoint another time for the meeting.[[50]](#cite_note-RA_3:6-63)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-n4_69-0)** According to the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"), the king died on 16 November, but the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") ("old style") was still used in Protestant Sweden at the time and the same date is still used now.
16. **[^](#cite_ref-n21_74-0)** Flag days are regulated by an ordinance issued by the [Government of Sweden](/wiki/Government_of_Sweden "Government of Sweden").[[58]](#cite_note-73) This means that the [national flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Sweden "Flag of Sweden") is flown on all public flag poles and buildings on those dates.
17. **[^](#cite_ref-n88_89-0)** The [Stockholm City Hall](/wiki/Stockholm_City_Hall "Stockholm City Hall"), built in 1927, has a similar spire with [Three Crowns](/wiki/Three_Crowns "Three Crowns") on its tower.
